topic: end of dry season — park visitor numbers dropping

turns.content: The park has been proper quiet lately. Wet season is in full swing and the visitor numbers have dropped right off. I go out on patrol some days and do not see a single person.
That must be a big shift from the busy season. Is the quiet a welcome change or does it create different challenges?

Bit of both. I prefer the quiet honestly. The country feels different when there are not hundreds of people moving through it every day. The animals are calmer. You can hear things. But the park needs the visitor numbers for funding so it is not simple.

That tension between conservation needs and tourism revenue is something a lot of national parks deal with. How significant is the drop at your park? Are we talking about half as many visitors, or almost none?

Almost none some weeks. The roads flood, tracks close, access points get cut off. February is the deepest part of the wet up here. The people who do come are serious, they know the conditions and they come prepared. No tour buses. No families stopping for selfies at the lookout.
So the visitor profile changes completely -- from casual tourists to experienced visitors who understand what they're getting into. Does the work shift for you as well during this period?

It does. Less managing people, more managing country. Maintenance, monitoring water levels, checking on infrastructure after storms. The waterfalls are running hard, the billabongs are full. There is a lot of water moving through this wanga and it reshapes things. Roads wash out. Signs get knocked over. Fences go down. The land does what it wants.

It sounds like the wet season creates a whole different kind of workload -- less public-facing but more physically demanding. How does the staffing work during the quiet period? Are there fewer rangers on, or does the maintenance work keep everyone busy?

Some staff take leave. Others get redeployed to other things. But there is always work. The quiet period is when we do the big maintenance jobs that we cannot do when visitors are everywhere. Repairing boardwalks, clearing tracks, fixing up the cultural sites. It is easier to work on the land when you are not working around people at the same time.

That makes sense -- using the low season for the heavy infrastructure work that would be disruptive during peak times. Is there any push from management to try to extend the tourist season or attract more wet season visitors?

There is always talk about that. Open things earlier, close things later, offer wet season experiences. And some of it is fine. Guided tours in the wet can be proper beautiful. The country is green and alive and the waterfalls are at their best. But there are limits. You cannot take people into areas where the roads are under a metre of water. The wet is the wet. You do not argue with it.

There's a practical ceiling on how much you can extend the season when the environment itself sets the boundaries. Are there other parks in the region competing for the same visitors during the dry season?

That is part of the problem. Uluru gets a lot of the marketing. The Kimberley has become popular. We are a bit forgotten up here sometimes even though the country is just as significant. The visitor numbers have dropped compared to ten or fifteen years ago. Management worries about it. I worry about different things.
What do you worry about, if not the numbers?

I worry about the country being looked after properly regardless of how many people come to see it. The cultural sites need maintenance whether there are visitors or not. The fire management needs to happen. The weed control. The feral animal work. That does not stop because the car parks are empty. But funding is tied to visitor numbers and when numbers drop the budget gets tighter.

That's a fundamental problem with funding conservation through tourism revenue -- the care the land needs is constant, but the income is seasonal and variable. Have there been any discussions about alternative funding models or supplementary revenue streams?

Some talk about carbon credits, research partnerships, that kind of thing. But it moves slow. Government decisions take time and this country does not wait for budget cycles. The weeds grow now. The ferals breed now. You cannot tell a buffalo to wait until the funding comes through.

That's a vivid way to put it. The mismatch between ecological timelines and bureaucratic timelines is a real problem across land management. In the meantime, it sounds like you and the other rangers are doing the work regardless, finding ways to manage with whatever resources you have.
We do what needs doing. That is the job. The country does not care about our budget. It just needs looking after. So we look after it. Quiet season or busy season, the work is the same underneath.

That kind of commitment -- doing the work because the land needs it, not because the numbers justify it -- is exactly what good land management looks like. I hope the funding catches up with the reality eventually.
